parts.name: Part 1
parts.instructions: You must answer this question. Write your answer in 220-260 words in an appropriate style.

tasks.lead: Your class has attended a seminar about how schools could improve students' media literacy. You have made the notes below. Write an essay discussing two of the ideas in your notes. You should explain which idea would be more effective, giving reasons to support your opinion.

stimulus.title: How could schools improve students' media literacy?
stimulus.body:
• teaching a dedicated course on evaluating online information
• building source-checking into every school subject
• inviting working journalists to explain how news is made

Some opinions expressed in the seminar:
"One weekly lesson cannot compete with six hours a day of scrolling."
"Checking sources should be as automatic as checking spelling."
"Until you see how a story is put together, you can't see how one is taken apart."

bullets: explain which would be more effective
give reasons to support your opinion

parts.name: Part 2
parts.instructions: Answer one of these questions. Write your answer in 220-260 words in an appropriate style.

tasks.lead: Your college principal wants to understand how students get their news, and has asked you to write a report based on a survey of your classmates.

stimulus.title: Report: how students follow the news
stimulus.body: Write a report summarising where students currently get their news and how much they trust it, identifying the main risks in these habits, and recommending how the college could respond.

bullets: where students get their news and how much they trust it
the main risks in these habits

tasks.lead: An English-language student magazine has invited reviews of documentaries about the media.

stimulus.title: Documentaries about the media
stimulus.body: Review a documentary that examines how the media or social media influence what people believe. Describe its central argument, assess how fairly it treated the subject, and say how watching it changed your own online habits, if at all.

bullets: how fairly it treated the subject
whether it changed your own habits

tasks.lead: Your students' union has funding for one awareness campaign this year and has invited proposals.

stimulus.title: Proposal: a campaign against misinformation
stimulus.body: Write a proposal for a campus campaign helping students recognise misinformation. Explain why the campaign is needed now, describe the activities it would involve, and explain why it deserves the funding more than other possible campaigns.

bullets: why the campaign is needed now
